title: FCC Commissioner Anna Gomez Will Fight for Press Freedom—Until Trump Fires Her
gist: FCC Commissioner Anna Gomez, the sole remaining Democrat on a 3-person commission, details how the Trump administration is weaponizing the FCC's broadcast license authority to pressure networks like ABC, CBS, and NBC into editorial capitulation—with the Disney/ABC case the clearest example. Gomez argues Disney will win on appeal because the administration's public record of retaliatory intent undermines any pretextual regulatory rationale. The Paramount-Skydance merger illustrated how license-approval power was used to extract concessions from CBS including eliminating DEI policies.

insights.claim: The FCC under Chairman Brendan Carr revived previously-dismissed broadcast complaints against ABC, CBS, and NBC that expert staff had already found invalid under existing law, a move Gomez characterizes as using regulatory pressure to force editorial changes rather than enforce genuine violations.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: Broadcast license holders ABC, CBS, NBC face ongoing regulatory overhang; investors in Disney, Paramount, and NBCUniversal parent companies should price in litigation costs and editorial-independence risk.
insights.quote: One of the first actions that this agency took in the broadcast area was it revived complaints against ABC, CBS, and NBC that the prior FCC staff, the expert staff, had dismissed as not raising valid complaints under the current law.
insights.locator: Gomez answer on when FCC began changing

insights.claim: The FCC called up all eight Disney-owned ABC affiliate broadcast licenses for early renewal—years before their 2028-2031 scheduled dates—as retaliation for ABC's refusal to fire Jimmy Kimmel and cancel The View, according to Gomez, who calls this 'the most egregious assault' on press freedom.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: Disney (DIS) faces material regulatory risk on its eight owned-and-operated broadcast licenses; the legal process could take years, creating sustained overhang on broadcast TV assets.
insights.quote: the commission basically out of the blue issued an order that said, 'No, we're gonna call up your licenses for renewal. You have 30 days to seek renewal of all eight of your licenses.'
insights.locator: Gomez answer on Disney affiliate license renewal

insights.claim: Gomez predicts Disney will ultimately win if the FCC takes adverse action on ABC's licenses and Disney appeals, because the administration has created a documented public record linking regulatory pressure directly to editorial demands—a record that makes the pretextual basis of the action transparent to courts.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: Markets may underweight Disney's legal position; if courts ultimately uphold First Amendment protections as Gomez expects, regulatory overhang could be lifted within 2-3 years.
insights.quote: if it gets appealed, they will win. Because what this administration is doing is contrary to the law, it's contrary to the Constitution, and it's very obvious.
insights.locator: Gomez answer on how Disney fight will turn out

themes.theme: Paramount settlement as regulatory extraction precedent

insights.claim: Paramount settled Trump's lawsuit against CBS—which Gomez views as legally baseless—because Paramount needed FCC approval to transfer licenses to Skydance, and the FCC approved the transfer just two days after the settlement, with CBS simultaneously committing to eliminate DEI policies and appoint an ombudsperson.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: The Paramount-Skydance precedent demonstrates that FCC license-transfer authority can function as a lever to extract editorial and corporate governance concessions; sets a template other networks will face.
insights.quote: Paramount did in fact settle that lawsuit because it wanted the FCC to approve the transfer of its licenses to Skydance. As soon as they settled that, two days later, the FCC approved the transfer.

insights.claim: Gomez raises national security concerns about the proposed Warner Bros. Discovery-Paramount merger because sovereign wealth funds from Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the UAE—at least one of which the US intelligence community confirmed ordered the killing of a journalist—will hold indirect ownership stakes in a combined entity that includes CBS News and CNN.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: Foreign ownership review of WBD-Paramount merger is now underway per Gomez; national security objections could delay or restructure the deal and create headline risk for both companies.
insights.quote: These governments are not friendly to press freedom. They use the media for geopolitical purposes... you don't think a call to the CEO is gonna make a difference about what gets produced, what doesn't get produced?
insights.locator: Gomez answer on Warner Bros.-Paramount merger

themes.theme: FCC quorum dynamics and presidential firing power

insights.claim: The FCC currently has only three commissioners (two Republicans, one Democrat) because two commissioners resigned last summer without replacement—Gomez suspects the White House deliberately left the seats vacant because it is easier to act with fewer dissenting votes.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: A structurally understaffed FCC is more susceptible to partisan capture; any appointment that restores full membership to 5 commissioners would shift the political dynamics of the agency.
insights.quote: I imagine it's much easier to deal with two colleagues than four.

insights.claim: Gomez believes Trump has not fired her because the FCC requires at least one non-Republican commissioner for a quorum to act, making any FCC actions taken without her potentially vulnerable to appeal—giving Trump a strategic incentive to retain her despite her vocal opposition.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: The Supreme Court's ruling in Trump v. Slaughter on presidential firing power for independent agency heads will directly determine whether Gomez can continue her opposition role.
insights.quote: I suspect that if he were to fire me, any actions the commission took would then probably be subject to appeal.
insights.locator: Gomez answer on why she hasn't been fired

insights.claim: The Supreme Court is expected imminently to rule in Trump v. Slaughter on whether the president can remove Federal Trade Commission—and by analogy FCC—commissioners without cause, which would fundamentally change the independence of all multi-member regulatory agencies.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: A ruling allowing at-will firing of independent commissioners would expose FTC, FCC, SEC, and CFTC to rapid political turnover—material risk for all regulated industries.
insights.quote: that's being litigated in the Supreme Court, which any day now, maybe any week now, we will see a decision in Trump v. Slaughter

themes.theme: Broadband access and AI economy divide

insights.claim: Gomez argues that the administration is spending tens of billions on fiber/satellite broadband infrastructure while simultaneously canceling digital equity programs and defunding affordable broadband initiatives, creating 'bridges to nowhere' in communities that can't afford or use the infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: Broadband utilization gaps could limit ROI on infrastructure spend and widen the digital divide entering the AI economy—a risk for ISPs and tech platforms dependent on user base expansion.
insights.quote: if we build out these tens of billions of dollars of fiber or satellite or fixed wireless services to households, and they don't know how to use it, or they can't afford to use it, then we're building bridges to nowhere.

themes.theme: Courts as last backstop against press censorship

insights.claim: Gomez observes that every time a media entity has appealed the administration's actions on First Amendment grounds—citing The New York Times and Wall Street Journal—courts have upheld free speech protections, but the administration keeps finding ways to circumvent individual rulings.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: Judicial protection is real but slow and piecemeal; companies that can sustain costly legal fights (Disney, major network parents) have a structural advantage over smaller broadcasters.
insights.quote: We have seen that when any entity appeals, the courts have upheld the First Amendment... The administration keeps trying, and they keep getting pushed back.
insights.locator: Gomez answer on next two and a half years

insights.claim: Gomez argues that Disney's decision to hire a top telecom attorney plus a former Solicitor General known for Supreme Court appellate work signals Disney is prepared to fight the license case to the highest court, significantly strengthening the network's legal position.
insights.speaker: Katie Drummond
insights.so_what: Disney's legal firepower reduces probability of a negotiated capitulation; sustained resistance by a large media company is the most credible deterrent to further FCC pressure on other networks.
insights.quote: their response not only had a top telecom attorney in DC, but also a former solicitor general, who is very well known, one of the top Supreme Court appellate lawyers.
insights.locator: Gomez answer on Disney's legal response
